title: Equal Groups Total Unknown
promptTemplate: {{subject}} places {{groupSize}} {{itemLabel}} in each {{groupLabel}}. There are {{groupCount}} {{groupLabelPlural}}. How many {{itemLabelPlural}} are there altogether?
explanationTemplate: This is an equal-groups multiplication problem. Multiply {{groupSize}} × {{groupCount}} to find the total number of {{itemLabelPlural}}.
mathSetupTemplate: Treat the problem as {{groupCount}} equal groups of {{groupSize}} {{itemLabelPlural}}.

choiceTemplates.rationale: Adds the values instead of multiplying equal groups.
Subtracts instead of finding the total across groups.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which values belong in the setup for this equal-groups problem?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use {{groupSize}} and {{groupCount}} because those are the two quantities the story gives you.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What operation should you use first?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use multiplication because {{groupSize}} is repeated in each of the {{groupCount}} groups.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What is the problem asking you to find?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Find the total number of {{itemLabelPlural}} altogether.

authorNotes: Best for arrays, trays, rows, teams, packs, and repeated equal sets.
Distractors should include addition and subtraction errors that look plausible to fourth graders.

title: Equal Groups Group Size Unknown
promptTemplate: {{groupCount}} {{groupLabelPlural}} share {{total}} {{itemLabelPlural}} equally. How many {{itemLabelPlural}} are in each {{groupLabel}}?
explanationTemplate: This is a division problem because a total of {{total}} is shared equally into {{groupCount}} groups. Divide {{total}} ÷ {{groupCount}}.
mathSetupTemplate: Use equal sharing with {{total}} split into {{groupCount}} equal groups.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which total and group count belong in the number sentence?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The total is {{total}} and it is shared into {{groupCount}} equal groups.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which operation matches equal sharing?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Division is correct because you are finding the amount in each group.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What does the answer represent?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The answer tells how many {{itemLabelPlural}} go in each {{groupLabel}}.

authorNotes: Use when the total and number of groups are known, but the amount in each group is unknown.

title: Equal Groups Number of Groups Unknown
promptTemplate: {{total}} {{itemLabelPlural}} are packed in groups of {{groupSize}}. How many {{groupLabelPlural}} can be filled?
explanationTemplate: Divide {{total}} by {{groupSize}} because you know the total and the size of each group. Then interpret the remainder based on the story.
mathSetupTemplate: Treat the problem as repeated groups of {{groupSize}} taken from a total of {{total}}.
translationTemplate: Compute {{total}} ÷ {{groupSize}} and use the remainder to answer the story question.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which total and group size matter here?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use {{total}} and {{groupSize}} because the story gives the whole amount and the size of one group.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which operation finds how many groups are needed?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Division finds the number of groups when you know the total and the amount in each group.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What must you decide after dividing?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: You must decide how the remainder affects the story. Sometimes the remainder means another group is needed.

authorNotes: Use for buses, bags, boxes, teams, or seating problems where remainders matter.

title: Decimal Combine Total
promptTemplate: {{subject}} buys one item for ${{amountA}} and another for ${{amountB}}. What is the total cost?
explanationTemplate: Add the two decimal amounts because the question asks for the total cost.
mathSetupTemplate: Line up the decimal points and add both amounts.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which money amounts should be added?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use ${{amountA}} and ${{amountB}} because those are the two prices in the story.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What operation finds the total cost?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Addition is correct because the question asks for the total amount together.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What does the answer represent?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The answer is the total cost of both items together.

authorNotes: Keep decimal places aligned and use money contexts for grade 4 relevance.

title: Decimal Change From Payment
promptTemplate: {{subject}} spends ${{costA}} and ${{costB}}, then pays with ${{payment}}. How much change should {{subjectPronoun}} get back?
explanationTemplate: First add the costs, then subtract that total from the payment amount to find the change.
mathSetupTemplate: Find the total spent first, then subtract from the amount paid.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which numbers are the costs and which number is the payment?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use ${{costA}} and ${{costB}} as the costs, then compare that total to the payment of ${{payment}}.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What operation finds the change after the total cost is known?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Subtraction finds what is left after paying for the items.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What does the final answer show?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The final answer is the amount of change returned after the purchase.

authorNotes: This is a two-step word problem: add first, subtract second.

title: Customary Measurement Conversion
explanationTemplate: Use the conversion fact {{conversionFact}} and multiply or divide based on the direction of the conversion.
mathSetupTemplate: Write the unit conversion first, then scale the amount.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which quantity and conversion fact matter here?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use {{quantity}} and the fact {{conversionFact}} because those are the values needed for the conversion.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Should you multiply or divide?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Choose the operation that moves from the starting unit to the target unit using the conversion fact.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What should the answer name?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The answer must be written in {{toUnitPlural}}, not the original units.

authorNotes: Use only grade 4 customary conversions already on the reference sheet.

title: Rectangle Area and Perimeter
promptTemplate: A rectangle has a length of {{length}} {{unitPlural}} and a width of {{width}} {{unitPlural}}. Which answer gives both its area and perimeter?
explanationTemplate: Area is length × width, and perimeter is length + length + width + width.
mathSetupTemplate: Use both rectangle formulas with the same length and width values.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which side lengths will you use in both formulas?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use length {{length}} and width {{width}} in both the area and perimeter calculations.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What operation gives the area first?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Area uses multiplication because it counts equal rows and columns of square units.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What must the correct answer include?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The correct choice must include both the area and the perimeter, not just one measurement.

authorNotes: Good distractors swap area and perimeter or compute only one of them correctly.

title: Angle Part-Whole Relationship
promptTemplate: A {{wholeAngle}}-degree angle is split into two angles. One angle is {{knownPart}} degrees. Which equation finds x, the other angle?
explanationTemplate: The two parts add to the whole angle, so use a part + part = whole equation.
mathSetupTemplate: Treat the unknown angle and known angle as two parts of the same whole angle.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Important values
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: Which angle measures belong in the equation?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: Use the whole angle of {{wholeAngle}} degrees and the known part of {{knownPart}} degrees.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Operation
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: How are the parts related to the whole angle?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: The angle parts add together to make the whole angle.

guidedStepTemplates.title: Main question
guidedStepTemplates.promptTemplate: What does x represent?
guidedStepTemplates.explanationTemplate: x is the missing part of the whole angle.

authorNotes: Useful for equation-writing and angle-decomposition items.
